data.history:
The Lakhmids were a pre-Islamic Arab dynasty that ruled the kingdom of al-Hirah in southern Mesopotamia from the late 3rd century until 602 CE. The dynasty was governed by the Nasrid ruling house of the Banu Lakhm tribe, which traced its lineage to the Qahtani confederation through Kahlan ibn Saba. The kingdom was founded by Amr ibn Adi (r. 293-302 CE), who appears in inscriptions as 'Amr ibn Lakhm' and established al-Hirah as the capital and political base. The Lakhmids functioned as a crucial client state of the Sassanian Empire, serving as a buffer between Persian and Roman spheres of influence and defending Persia's western frontier against nomadic Arab tribal incursions.

The kingdom emerged after the collapse of the Kingdom of Hatra and Palmyrene Empire, filling the resulting power vacuum in the region. Initially pagan, the Lakhmids gradually adopted Christianity, with Christianity being introduced to al-Hirah in the 4th century and the city having a bishop by 410 CE. The dynasty reached its zenith under al-Mundhir III ibn al-Nu'man (r. 505-554 CE), who reigned for approximately fifty years and achieved significant military victories, including defeating the Byzantine-allied Ghassanids at the Battle of Callinicum in 531 CE. During this period, the Lakhmids projected Sassanian hegemony across eastern Arabia, extending their influence into regions that would later become modern Gulf states.

The kingdom maintained sophisticated Persian-influenced court culture while serving as a counterbalance to the Ghassanids, the main Arab client kingdom allied with the Romans. Al-Hirah became a major center for trade and missionary activity, acting as a gateway for Christian missions to both the Sassanian world and the desert Arabs. The last Lakhmid king, al-Nu'man III ibn al-Mundhir (r. 580-602 CE), converted to Christianity, reportedly influenced by Simeon Jabara, the bishop of al-Hirah. However, this conversion was viewed unfavorably by the Sassanians, who made efforts to reconvert him.

The dynasty came to an abrupt end in 602 CE when Sassanian emperor Khosrow II deposed and executed al-Nu'man III, bringing Lakhmid territories under direct Persian control. This dissolution proved strategically disastrous for the Sassanians, as it removed their crucial Arab buffer state and inadvertently paved the way for the Arab Muslim conquest of Iraq in the 630s under Khalid ibn al-Walid. The fall of the Lakhmids was followed by the Banu Bakr's victory over the Sassanians at the Battle of Dhi Qar and ultimately contributed to the decisive Battle of Qadisiyya in 636 and the Muslim conquest of Persia. Lakhmid descendants later established the Abbadid dynasty in 11th-century al-Andalus, while other branches settled across Syria, Palestine, and Egypt during the Islamic conquests.

folkLegends.title: The Architect's Sacrifice at Khawarnaq Palace
folkLegends.story: According to later tradition, the legendary Byzantine architect Cenmar was recruited by the Lakhmid court to design the construction of the famous Khawarnaq Palace near al-Hirah, built by Al-Nu'man I to accommodate the young Sassanian prince Bahram V. After completing the magnificent design, Cenmar was killed to prevent him from designing a similar castle for another person.

data.nameEtymology: The name 'Lakhmid' derives from the Banu Lakhm tribe, appearing in the late 3rd century Paikuli inscription which refers to 'Amr of the Lakhm' as one of the vassals of the Sassanian Empire. However, historians note there is no evidence that any connection between Nasrid leaders and Lakhm that existed in the 3rd century was still present in the 6th century.

Diaspora of Lakhmid descendants during and after Islamic conquests, with some establishing the Abbadid dynasty in 11th-century Seville

data.history:
The Manasir al-Nil (Riverain Manasir) are a Sudanese Arab riverine tribe inhabiting the Fourth Cataract region of the Nile in Northern Sudan, calling their homeland Dar al-Manasir. This fertile river oasis extends approximately 130 kilometers along the Nile between the upstream Rubatab tribe near Abu Hamed and the downstream Shaiqiyah tribe past Birti village. The tribe represents an indigenous Nile culture that adopted Islam and speaks Arabic as their first language, while maintaining a dual lifestyle that combines settled riverine agriculture with seasonal nomadic pastoralism in the adjacent Bayuda Desert.

The current Manasir community formed through voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous mostly Nubian groups, descendants of emigrating Arabs, and recruited tribal minorities. By recognizing a common genealogical pedigree, all members establish mutual rights and obligations, uniting themselves in land claims against neighboring tribes. Earlier travelers witnessed Beja and Bisharin influences from the Red Sea Hills among the Manasir, and strong similarities of their burial customs with Nubians can still be observed.

The tribe is organizationally divided into two main branches: Riverain Manasir (Manasir al-Nil) comprising ten clans including al-Wahabab, al-Suleimaniyah, al-Kabanah, al-Diqeisab, al-Hamsab, al-Ga'al, al-'Ababsah, al-Farei'ab, al-Hamdatiab, and al-'Amasib; and Bedouin Manasir (Manasir al-Badiyah) consisting of four clans: al-Khabra, al-Hamamir, al-Muleikab, and al-Kagbab who share grazing grounds in the Bayuda Desert. During dry seasons, some clans migrate to the desert area of the Kababish tribe to the west, others to the grazing grounds of Wad Hamid in the Ga'ali Country or to the Rubatab Country.

The riverain Manasir pursue small-scale agriculture on alluvial soils in the immediate vicinity of the Nile, with dates as their primary cash crop sold on the national market. They are renowned for their skill in building mud houses (Galus) and historically floated wood from the Atbarah region. The 1993 population was estimated at 30,000 residents with an additional 17,000 non-resident Manasir, though these figures remain vague regarding Bedouin populations. The community faced displacement due to the Merowe Dam project flooding their ancestral homeland.

data.history: Based on the existing data, the Misaid are documented as a subsection of the larger Bani Kaab tribal confederation, which has historical presence in Oman and the United Arab Emirates. The Bani Kaab were traditionally associated with territories around and north of the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah, as well as areas east of Buraimi including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta and Wadi Qor. By the turn of the 20th century, the Misaid had adopted a settled lifestyle, unlike some other nomadic Bani Kaab subsections such as the Drisah and Shwaihiyin, becoming part of a total Bani Kaab population of approximately 7,250 members. The tribe's political allegiances shifted significantly during the 19th and 20th centuries, reflecting the fluid nature of tribal alliances in southeastern Arabia. In 1844, they participated alongside other Bani Kaab sections in an alliance with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of the Bani Yas confederation, successfully driving Wahhabi forces from the Buraimi region. However, by the 1940s, tribal loyalties had transformed dramatically when the Bani Kaab, under their sheikh Obaid bin Jumah, declared allegiance to Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province. During the 1950s Buraimi Dispute, the Misaid, as part of the broader Bani Kaab structure, supported Saudi claims to the region and actively opposed British forces and the Trucial Oman Levies. This involvement in regional geopolitical conflicts positioned the Misaid within the larger struggles that ultimately shaped modern Gulf state boundaries and tribal allegiances in the UAE-Oman border regions.

data.history: The research provided contains no information about Bani Abdullah of the Mutayr tribal confederation. While the documents extensively cover other Arabian tribes including Bani Shaiba (custodians of the Kaaba from Quraysh), Bani Kaab (from Oman and UAE), various Yemeni tribal confederations like Hamdan, Kinda, Madhhaj, and Himyar, and early Islamic migration patterns, there is no mention of Bani Abdullah as a branch of Mutayr anywhere in the source material. The research confirms the existence of major tribal structures throughout the Arabian Peninsula and provides detailed genealogical information for numerous tribes, but does not contain any specific historical, genealogical, or geographical information about this particular tribal branch. Without direct research evidence about Bani Abdullah's origins, migrations, conflicts, territorial control, or historical development within the Mutayr confederation, no verified historical narrative can be constructed beyond acknowledging the absence of documentation in these sources.

Qahtān is an ancient Arab tribal confederation representing one of the two major divisions of Arab peoples, tracing its lineage to Qahtani origins as opposed to the Adnaniyah northern Arabs. According to genealogical tradition, Qahtan was among the earliest Arabs, with his descendants considered the authentic ancient Arabs of southern Arabia. The confederation is composed of three main tribes: Sanhan, Junb, and Rufaida, with the tribe's original homeland in Yemen from where they later expanded northward. The research indicates that according to Arab genealogical beliefs, Arabs divide themselves into 'Arab 'Ariba' (true Arabs) who descend from their greatest ancestor Qahtan, 'Arab Ba'ida' (extinct Arabs) mentioned in the Quran as having perished due to their disbelief, and 'Arab Musta'riba' (Arabized Arabs) whose greatest ancestor was Adnan.

During the medieval period, the Qahtan tribe achieved significant prominence in the Najd region, particularly settling in Al-Quwai'iyah and expanding their influence throughout southern Najd. However, when Najd came under the rule of Ibn Saud during the second Saudi state, the tribe eventually returned to its original homeland in the south. Several historians describe this period as when Qahtan became one of the powerful tribes of Najd for a time before departing for their ancestral lands. The confederation demonstrates complex genealogical connections within the broader Qahtani tribal system, with the research noting that the Harb tribe traces its lineage as Harb ibn Saad ibn Saad ibn Khawlan ibn Amr ibn Qadha'ah ibn Himyar ibn Qahtan.

The Ya'rubids of Oman, who ruled between 1624 and 1742, traced their descent from Ya'arab bin Kahtan, whom some date to about 800 BC, demonstrating the far-reaching influence of Qahtani lineages. The family originated in Yemen and belonged to the Ghafiri faction before establishing their rule in Oman. Today, members of the Qahtan confederation and its sub-tribes are distributed across the Arabian Peninsula, including Yemen, Saudi Arabia, Oman, Qatar, Iraq, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates. The tribe has produced numerous notable figures in modern times, including politicians, athletes, academics, and religious leaders, maintaining their historical significance in contemporary Arabian Peninsula society.

data.history:
The Ramzan are a sub-tribal section of the larger Na'im tribe, one of the most significant traditionally Ghafiri Arab tribes in the Gulf region. As part of the Na'im confederation, they share the broader tribal identity and Adnani lineage. According to J.G. Lorimer's Gazetteer of the Persian Gulf published in 1904, the Na'im were described as 'a Bedouin tribe who grazed their cattle on pastures surrounding Zubarah in 1873.' The tribe were reported as being one of the most powerful tribes in Qatar in an 1890 British government report, with 60 or 70 of the tribe's branch in Qatar having a hereditary attachment to the Bahraini emir.

In 1937, a critical dispute over Zubarah took place between Bahrain and Qatar that fundamentally affected the Ramzan section's distinct identity. The Na'im in Qatar split into two sections during this conflict: the Ramzan, who supported the Qatari emir Abdullah bin Jassim Al Thani, and the main section Al Jabr, who had pledged allegiance to the ruler of Bahrain. This political division marked the Ramzan's emergence as a distinct identity within the broader Na'im confederation, differentiating them from their tribal kinsmen who maintained loyalty to Bahrain.

Hostilities escalated when an armed force sent by Abdullah Al Thani, numbering in the hundreds, confronted around 60 armed men of the Na'im who were encamped inside Ath Thaqab Fort. The conflict resulted in casualties and the capture of the fort, with Abdullah Al Thani's forces occupying the villages of Ath Thaqab, Freiha, Al-ʽArish and Al Khuwayr. As a result of this conflict, about 1,000 members of the Na'im tribe went into exile in Bahrain, with some returning after the Second World War. The Ramzan section, having chosen to support Qatar, remained in the peninsula and became integrated into Qatar's tribal demographic structure.

As part of the Na'im's traditional territory, the Ramzan shared in the tribe's historical presence around the Buraimi oasis and their role as one of the numerically strongest tribes in the region, with the combined Na'im population estimated at approximately 13,000 at the beginning of the twentieth century. The Qatar section represents families who settled in the peninsula, contributing to Qatar's tribal demographic structure while maintaining traditional tribal customs and adapting to modern Qatari society. Their connection to geographical features is evidenced by places like Waab Bin Ramzan, a prominent depression near Al Thakhira that was named after 'a man of the Al Ramzan tribe who is said to have died there long ago.'

folkLegends.title: The Man of Waab Bin Ramzan
folkLegends.story: A prominent depression near Al Thakhira earned its name from a man of the Al Ramzan tribe who is said to have died there long ago. The term waab refers to a low-lying, expansive, and moisture-retaining area where rainwater would accumulate for several weeks, creating a temporary wetland that later became fertile ground for various wild plants and grasses.
folkLegends.source: local oral tradition recorded in Al Thakhira historical accounts

data.history:
The Sabaeans were an ancient South Arabian civilization that emerged around 1200-1000 BCE in the highlands of Yemen, centered at Ma'rib with their capital and the famous dam that sustained their agricultural economy. According to Arab genealogical tradition, they descended from Saba, son of Qahtan, making them part of the Qahtani Arab lineage and considered ancestors of many modern Qahtani tribes. The kingdom experienced two distinct periods: the first Sabaean kingdom (8th-1st centuries BCE) dominated by rulers called 'mukarrib' (federators) who established hegemony over neighboring South Arabian kingdoms, and a second resurgent kingdom (1st-3rd centuries CE) with rulers using the title 'malik' (kings).

During their imperial peak under Karib'il Watar (8th-7th centuries BCE), the Sabaeans controlled vast territories extending from Najran in the north to the Gulf of Aden, dominating the lucrative frankincense and myrrh trade routes that connected the Mediterranean and Indian Ocean networks. Their advanced irrigation system centered on the Ma'rib Dam supported a 10,000-hectare oasis and sustained their economy for over a millennium. The Sabaeans also established significant presence in the Horn of Africa, founding the kingdom of D'mt in Ethiopia around 800 BCE and maintaining trade networks along the East African coast.

The first Sabaean kingdom declined due to Roman economic pressure and territorial losses to neighboring kingdoms like Ma'in, Qataban, and ultimately Himyar, which annexed them around the 1st century BCE. After briefly re-emerging around 100 CE with Sanaa as a secondary capital, the Sabaeans were permanently conquered by the Himyarites around 275 CE. Following the collapse of the Ma'rib Dam in the 6th century CE, the event known as 'Sil al-Aram' (the Great Flood) caused massive population dispersal throughout Arabia. Sabaean tribal descendants migrated both internally within Yemen and externally to various regions, becoming ancestors to numerous Qahtani tribes found throughout the modern Arabian Peninsula, particularly in the GCC states where their lineage is still recognized today.

folkLegends.title: Queen of Sheba and King Solomon
folkLegends.story: According to biblical and Quranic tradition, the Queen of Sheba (known as Bilqis in Islamic tradition and Makeda in Ethiopian tradition) visited King Solomon of Israel, engaging in trade involving aromatics and gold. The story appears in both the Hebrew Bible and the Quran, though historians consider the account legendary as early first-millennium BCE epigraphic sources show no evidence of diplomatic missions or women rulers in Saba during that period.

folkLegends.title: Sil al-Aram (The Great Flood)
folkLegends.story: The catastrophic collapse of the Ma'rib Dam caused widespread flooding that destroyed surrounding villages, cities, and farms, forcing the Sabaean population to migrate throughout Arabia. This event, mentioned in the Quran, led to the dispersal of Sabaean tribes across the Arabian Peninsula, with many becoming ancestors of modern tribal groups.

data.nameEtymology: The name 'Saba' appears to derive from the ancient South Arabian kingdom centered at Ma'rib. Among South Arabians and Abyssinians, Sheba's name carried prestige as it was widely considered the birthplace of South Arabian civilization. The term appears in various forms across ancient sources including Sabaic inscriptions and external accounts.

The Sudan tribe (Arabic: السودان, singular Al Suwaidi Arabic: السويدي) is an Arab tribe primarily settled in the United Arab Emirates, Qatar, and other Gulf states, traditionally serving as close associates of the Bani Yas of Abu Dhabi. At the turn of the 20th century, the settled Sudan numbered approximately 5,000 people distributed across the Trucial States with 405 houses in Abu Dhabi and Batin, 250 houses in Dubai, 300 in Sharjah, and 12 in Ajman. They also established island settlements with 20 houses on Abu Musa and 40 families on Sirri Island. The tribe primarily subsisted on fishing and pearling, notably distinguishing themselves from other tribal groups by not owning date gardens in the inland oases. Members of the Sudan from Abu Dhabi founded Bida'a (modern-day Doha) on Qatar's eastern coast in 1766, establishing one of the earliest Arab settlements in the area. The tribe played significant roles in regional politics through their alliance with the ruling Al Nahyan family of Abu Dhabi, particularly when Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan's mother was a Suwaidi and he married the daughter of Sultan bin Nasir Al Suwaidi, the Sheikh of the Sudan. The Sudan were frequently involved in the complex territorial politics of the 19th century Gulf, including conflicts over strategic locations like Deira in Dubai and proposed settlements at Al Zorah in Ajman. In Qatar, the tribe faced multiple challenges including British bombardments in 1801 and 1841 due to suspicions of piracy, with the 1841 incident resulting in fines and the eventual displacement of the near-bankrupt Sudan from Bida'a in 1843 when Isa bin Tarif of the Al Bin Ali tribe forced them to relocate to Lingah.

data.history: The Thyni were an ancient Thracian tribe that inhabited southeastern Thrace in the Balkans, not the Arabian Peninsula. According to historical sources, they were closely related to the Bithynians and were praised by Xenophon as formidable warriors who were 'supposed to be the most dangerous of all the tribes, especially at night fighting.' The Thyni included clubs among their weapons and often served as mercenaries in organized armies. During the early Iron Age, following the Bronze Age collapse, a section of the Thyni migrated alongside the Bithynians from Thrace to Asia Minor, where they expelled or subdued the Mysians, Caucones and other minor tribes. The region they settled became known as Thynia, named after their prominence there, while the adjacent area became Bithynia after their Bithynian allies. Both tribes were later incorporated into the Lydian monarchy under King Croesus around 550 BCE, and subsequently became part of the Persian satrapy of Phrygia when Lydia fell to Cyrus the Great in 546 BCE. This represents an ancient Thracian tribal entity with no connection to Arabian Peninsula genealogy or Gulf tribal structures.

migrationPath.description: Migration from Thrace to Asia Minor during early Iron Age, following Bronze Age collapse

data.history: The Yammahi are one of the three notable sections of the Sharqiyin tribe, alongside the Hafaitat and Hamudiyin. The Sharqiyin were traditionally a Ghafiri-aligned tribe and the dominant force along the east coast of the Trucial States, known as Shamailiyah, being the second most numerous tribe in the area by the early 19th century. The Yammahi section, along with other Sharqiyin groups, settled extensively along the east coast from Kalba to Dibba, as well as in the Wadi Ham and Jiri plain. By the turn of the 20th century, the entire Sharqiyin confederation was approximately 7,000 strong. The Yammahi historically populated the mountainous areas around Jabal Yibir (Jabal Al-Mebrah) and the Wadi Awsaq region in what is now Fujairah and Ras Al Khaimah. They were part of the broader Sharqiyin struggle for independence from Sharjah rule, with multiple rebellions occurring in 1866, 1876, 1879, and 1884, ultimately achieving practical independence in 1901 under the leadership of the Hafaitat section's Sheikh Hamad bin Abdullah Al Sharqi. Today, the Al Yammahi continue to be one of the prominent tribal populations in Dibba Al-Fujairah and maintain their presence in the mountainous regions of Fujairah emirate.

timelineEvents.title: First Sharqiyin Rebellion
timelineEvents.description: The Sharqiyin, including the Yammahi section, first withheld tribute to Sharjah on the death of Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi, when their headman Abdullah bin Khamis Al Sharqi rebelled

timelineEvents.title: Second Sharqiyin Rebellion
timelineEvents.description: Another rebellion took place when the Al Qasimi Wali of Dibba jailed twelve Sharqiyin, leading to a Sharqiyin/Shihu alliance against Sharjah forces

timelineEvents.title: Hamad bin Abdullah's Insurrection
timelineEvents.description: Sheikh Hamad bin Abdullah Al Sharqi led an insurrection against Sheikh Saqr bin Khalid Al Qasimi of Sharjah, taking Fujairah Fort before being forced into exile in Muscat

timelineEvents.title: Fourth Sharqiyin Rebellion
timelineEvents.description: Hamad bin Abdullah led another rebellion against Sharjah, taking control of Ghurfa and the strategic Bithnah Fort, leading to Sharjah's loss of effective control over the east coast

timelineEvents.title: Declaration of Independence
timelineEvents.description: The Sharqiyin, including the Yammahi section, achieved practical independence from Sharjah under Sheikh Hamad bin Abdullah Al Sharqi's leadership

data.history: The Banu Abs are an ancient Arabian tribe belonging to the Northern Adnanite Arabs, with their genealogical line traced as: Abs ibn Baghid ibn Rayth ibn Ghatafan ibn Sa'd ibn Qays ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan. Originally inhabiting the northern Hejaz region around what is today called Hurrat Bani Rasheed near Medina, they formed a branch of the powerful Ghatafan confederation and were recognized as one of the Jamarat of the Arabs - the most powerful tribes that took no allegiance to anyone but themselves. The tribe became legendary for their role in the famous 40-year War of Dahis and al-Ghabra against their brother tribe Banu Dhubyan in the 6th century. This conflict, named after the racing horses Dahis (belonging to Qays ibn Zuhayr of Banu Abs) and al-Ghabra (belonging to Hudhayfah ibn Badr of Banu Fazara), began over a disputed horse race and escalated when Qays's brother Malik was killed by Ibn Badr. The tribe produced the renowned warrior-poet Antarah ibn Shaddad (c. 525-615), author of one of the Mu'allaqat and protagonist of the epic romance 'Antar and Abla'. The Banu Abs gained significant political influence during the Umayyad period through the marriage of Caliph Abd al-Malik (r. 685-705) to the Absi noblewoman Wallada, whose sons al-Walid I and Sulayman became caliphs. Her relatives, particularly al-Qa'qa ibn Khulayd, established the town of Hiyar Bani Qa'qa in northwestern Syria and held important governorships. By the Fatimid period, parts of the tribe were expelled to Upper Egypt around 975-996 CE for supporting the Qarmatians. Modern descendants are widely dispersed, with the Bani Rasheed (tracing to 7th-century warrior Rasheed Al-Zaul Al-Absi) found across Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, UAE, Sudan, and Eritrea, while the Al Hubus (Habsi) tribe dominates eastern Oman and parts of UAE, maintaining their reputation for courage and tribal solidarity.

folkLegends.title: The Prophet Khalid ibn Sinan
folkLegends.story: Absi traditions tell of their prophet Khalid ibn Sinan who taught them Biblical monotheism, and the worship of God as 'al-ahad al-samad', prior to Muhammad. It was told that Khalid saved his tribe (accounts differ on how) and that most men of the Abs rejected this prophet at the time. Some have claimed that Khalid prophesied the 'last' prophet after him, who in Islam is Muhammad.

folkLegends.title: Sasanian Battle Tradition
folkLegends.story: Their traditions recall 3,000 of them repelling a Sasanian attack of 20,000.

folkLegends.title: King Zuhayr's Prophecy
folkLegends.story: Another tradition has it that King Zuhayr had predicted Muhammad, but Zuhayr is not called prophet for that.

data.nameEtymology: Named after their eponymous ancestor Abs ibn Baghid

data.history: Al Sulaimi are a tribal branch descended from the larger Bani Sulaim confederation, tracing their lineage to Sulaim ibn Mansour from the Qays Aylan branch within the Adnani genealogical tree. According to Islamic tradition, this places them among the Adnanite Arabs who trace their descent through Adnan to Ishmael, son of Abraham, distinguishing them from the Qahtanite Arabs of southern Arabia. The Qays Aylan were a significant tribal confederation within the Mudar branch of Adnanites, historically inhabiting northern, western, and central Arabia. Research reveals a particularly significant connection between Al Sulaimi and the Al Bin Ali tribe, who used the distinctive Al Sulaimi Flag until the 1960s across Bahrain, Qatar, and Kuwait. This flag, consisting of four red and three white stripes with seven red triangles facing west, became a symbol of considerable political importance throughout the Gulf region and notably influenced the current flags of Bahrain and Qatar. The Al Bin Ali, who were associated with the Utub confederation, were politically important and moved between Qatar and Bahrain, becoming the original dominant group in the Zubarah area. They were renowned for their wealth, courage, and practical independence in Bahrain, where they maintained self-governance and were uniquely exempt from paying taxes to the ruling Al Khalifa. The Al Sulaimi flag held deep cultural significance and was prominently displayed on ships during wartime and pearl diving seasons, as well as during special occasions including weddings, Eid celebrations, and war dances (Ardha). The flag's political importance extended beyond the Al Bin Ali, as it was also used by the Al-Khalifa and Al-Sabah ruling families when they were in Kuwait during the mid-eighteenth century, demonstrating the widespread recognition and respect for Al Sulaimi identity across the Gulf region.

timelineEvents.title: Al Sulaimi Flag Usage by Ruling Families
timelineEvents.description: The Al Sulaimi flag was used by Al-Khalifa and Al-Sabah ruling families when they were in Kuwait during the mid-eighteenth century

timelineEvents.title: End of Al Sulaimi Flag Usage
timelineEvents.description: The Al Bin Ali tribe discontinued the use of the Al Sulaimi Flag after using it across Bahrain, Qatar, and Kuwait
